headline: National Harmony Bill Tabled and Withdrawn 3 Times Since 2014
context: The National Harmony and Reconciliation Bill — designed to replace the Sedition Act with a modern framework — has been tabled and withdrawn from Parliament 3 times since 2014.

cards.big: The National Harmony Bill has been written, tabled, and withdrawn 3 times.
cards.sub: Each withdrawal followed objections that the bill would 'weaken protections for Malay-Muslim identity.'

cards.big: The bill proposed replacing criminal sedition with mediation-based conflict resolution.
cards.sub: International best practice supports restorative over punitive approaches to inter-community tensions.

cards.big: 58 countries have national harmony or anti-discrimination legislation.
cards.sub: Malaysia is the only upper-middle-income country in ASEAN without one.

cards.big: The bill had support from 67% of parliamentarians in informal polling.
cards.sub: Formal support evaporated when lobbying intensified. The gap between private and public positions is 67%.

cards.big: A bill supported by 67% of MPs in private and zero times in public. Fear of being seen to vote for harmony is the problem.

A National Harmony Act would provide the legal infrastructure for managing inter-ethnic and inter-religious tensions constructively. Its repeated withdrawal shows the political cost of unity legislation exceeds its political reward.
